title: Conversational UI Patterns
description: Learn the fundamental patterns for designing conversational interfaces — from chat UX to voice interactions to multimodal experiences.
learningObjectives: Identify and apply the core structural elements of conversational interfaces including message types, input mechanisms, and visual hierarchy
Design conversation flows that guide users effectively using linear, branching, and open-ended patterns
Choose the right input mechanisms and message formats for different conversational contexts and user needs

sections.title: Anatomy of a Conversation UI
sections.content:
A conversation UI looks simple on the surface — messages go back and forth. But beneath that simplicity is a carefully designed system of visual hierarchy, timing, and spatial relationships that make the interaction feel natural.

**The Message Thread**

The message thread is the backbone of any conversational interface. It's where the entire interaction history lives, and its design choices affect how users perceive and navigate the conversation.

• **Visual distinction between participants** — Users need to instantly tell their messages apart from the AI's responses. This is typically achieved through alignment (user messages on the right, AI on the left), color (different bubble colors), or both. The key is consistency — once you establish a visual language, never break it.
• **Timestamps and grouping** — Not every message needs a visible timestamp. Group messages sent within a short window and show a single timestamp for the group. For longer conversations, date separators ("Today," "Yesterday") help users orient themselves.
• **Scroll behavior** — New messages should auto-scroll to the bottom, but only when the user is already at the bottom of the thread. If they've scrolled up to review earlier messages, auto-scrolling is disruptive. Show a "New messages" indicator instead.

**The Input Area**

The input area is where users compose their messages, and its design signals what kind of input the system expects.

• **Text input sizing** — A single-line input suggests short queries. A multi-line text area invites longer, more detailed messages. Match the input size to your use case: a quick-answer bot gets a single line, a writing assistant gets a text area.
• **Send affordance** — The send button should be visually prominent and accessible. Support keyboard shortcuts (Enter to send, Shift+Enter for new line) and make the convention clear. Nothing frustrates users more than accidentally sending half a message.
• **Input state indicators** — Show users when the AI is processing their message (a typing indicator), when the input is disabled (during generation), and when they can type again. These states prevent the confusion of sending messages into a void.

**The System Layer**

Beyond messages, conversational UIs need system-level elements that frame the interaction.

• **Welcome state** — What does the user see before any messages are exchanged? A blank chat feels cold. A welcome message with suggested prompts or a brief description of capabilities helps users start confidently.
• **Status indicators** — Connection status, AI availability, and processing state should be visible but not distracting. A subtle status dot or banner is usually sufficient.
• **Conversation management** — For multi-conversation interfaces, users need ways to start new conversations, switch between existing ones, and find past interactions. Search and conversation titles become essential at scale.

sections.title: Message Types and Formatting
sections.content:
Conversational interfaces aren't limited to plain text bubbles. Different message types serve different communication needs, and choosing the right format for each piece of information makes the difference between a clear conversation and a confusing wall of text.

**Text Messages**

Text is the foundation of conversational UI, but even plain text benefits from thoughtful formatting:

• **Markdown rendering** — Support bold, italic, headers, and lists in AI responses. Claude naturally produces well-structured text with headers and bullet points. Rendering this formatting makes longer responses scannable and organized.
• **Code blocks** — If your AI assistant helps with technical tasks, render code in monospaced font with syntax highlighting. Include a "Copy" button — users almost always want to copy code snippets.
• **Message length handling** — Very long messages need special treatment. Consider collapsible sections, a "Show more" truncation, or breaking long responses into multiple message bubbles. A 2,000-word response in a single bubble is overwhelming.

**Rich Media Messages**

Some information is better communicated through structured visual elements rather than text:

• **Cards** — Structured containers with an image, title, description, and action buttons. Ideal for product recommendations, search results, or any content with a consistent structure. Cards turn text-heavy responses into scannable visual elements.
• **Carousels** — Horizontally scrollable sets of cards. Useful when presenting multiple options ("Here are 5 restaurants near you"). Keep carousels short — more than 7-8 items becomes unwieldy.
• **Tables** — For data comparisons or structured information, render tables instead of describing data in prose. "Product A costs $10 and has 4.5 stars" is better as a table when comparing multiple products.
• **Images and media** — When the AI references visual content, embed it directly in the conversation rather than linking out. Inline images, charts, and diagrams maintain conversational flow.

**Interactive Messages**

Some messages invite user action beyond reading:

• **Forms within messages** — Short forms (a rating scale, a date picker, a dropdown) can be embedded directly in the conversation. This is faster than asking users to type structured information.
• **Confirmation messages** — When the AI is about to take an action, show a confirmation card with the action details and "Confirm" / "Cancel" buttons. This pattern is critical for any destructive or irreversible operation.
• **Feedback prompts** — After completing a task, prompt users to rate the interaction. Keep it lightweight — a thumbs up/down or a single-question survey embedded in the conversation.

sections.title: Input Mechanisms
sections.content:
How users provide input shapes the entire conversational experience. The right input mechanism reduces friction, guides users toward successful interactions, and prevents the blank-page paralysis that open-ended text boxes can create.

**Free Text Input**

Open text input is the most flexible but also the most intimidating input mechanism.

• **When to use it** — Free text works best for experienced users, complex queries, or creative tasks where you can't predict what the user wants to say. It's the default for general-purpose assistants.
• **Placeholder text matters** — The placeholder in your text input is a design opportunity. "Ask me anything" is vague. "Ask about your sales data, team performance, or upcoming deadlines" is specific and actionable. The placeholder should answer the question: "What should I type here?"
• **Input validation and guidance** — If your AI feature expects a certain type of input, validate before sending. If the user pastes a 50,000-word document into a field that handles 1,000 words, catch that before it fails on the backend.

**Suggested Responses and Quick Replies**

Suggested responses are pre-written options that users can tap instead of typing.

• **When to use them** — Suggestions shine when there's a predictable set of responses ("Yes / No / Tell me more"), when users are on mobile (typing is slower), or when you want to guide users toward productive interactions.
• **Design considerations** — Display suggestions as horizontally scrollable chips or vertically stacked buttons. Keep text short (2-5 words per suggestion). Remove suggestions once the user makes a choice or types their own response.
• **Dynamic suggestions** — The most effective suggestions change based on context. After Claude answers a question, offer follow-up suggestions based on what the user is likely to ask next: "Go deeper," "Give me an example," "How does this compare to...?"

**Structured Input**

Sometimes you need specific, structured information from users.

• **Inline forms** — When the AI needs specific data points (a date range, a budget, a location), embed a small form in the conversation rather than asking users to type each field separately. Three separate messages asking for departure city, arrival city, and dates is worse than a single flight search form.
• **File and image upload** — If your AI feature can process documents, images, or data files, make upload seamless. Drag-and-drop, paste from clipboard, and file picker should all work. Show a preview of what was uploaded so users can confirm.
• **Voice input** — For mobile or hands-free contexts, offer speech-to-text input. Show the transcription in real time so users can catch errors before sending. Always provide a text fallback — voice input in a quiet office or public space isn't always practical.

**Choosing the Right Mechanism**

The best conversational interfaces mix input mechanisms based on context. Start a conversation with suggested prompts, switch to free text for the main interaction, and use quick replies for follow-up decisions. The input mechanism should adapt to the conversation, not force the conversation to adapt to the input.

sections.title: Conversation Flow Patterns
sections.content:
The flow of a conversation — how it moves from one state to the next — is the invisible architecture of conversational design. Different tasks call for different flow patterns, and choosing the wrong pattern makes even a capable AI feel frustrating to use.

**Linear Flows**

Linear flows move through a predefined sequence of steps, one at a time.

• **Best for** — Onboarding, form completion, step-by-step wizards, or any process with a fixed sequence. "First, tell me your name. Next, what role are you in? Finally, what are you hoping to learn?"
• **Design principles** — Show progress through the sequence (step 2 of 5), allow going back to previous steps, and keep each step focused on a single question or action. Don't ask three things at once in a linear flow.
• **When to break linearity** — If a user's answer to step 2 makes step 3 irrelevant, skip it. Rigid linear flows that ask unnecessary questions feel robotic.

**Branching Flows**

Branching flows change direction based on user responses, creating different paths through the conversation.

• **Best for** — Troubleshooting, decision support, recommendation engines, or any scenario where different inputs lead to fundamentally different outcomes. "Are you having a billing issue or a technical issue?" leads to entirely different conversation paths.
• **Design principles** — Map out the decision tree before designing the UI. Identify dead ends and loops. Every branch should lead somewhere useful — if a branch ends with "I can't help with that," you've designed a trap, not a conversation.
• **Graceful branch recovery** — Users will try to jump between branches. If someone starts down the billing path but then mentions a technical issue, the AI should be able to pivot without forcing the user to start over.

**Open-Ended Flows**

Open-ended flows have no predetermined path — the conversation goes wherever the user takes it.

• **Best for** — General-purpose assistants, creative brainstorming, exploratory research, or any context where you genuinely can't predict what users will want to discuss.
• **Design challenges** — Without structure, users can feel lost. Combat this with suggested topics, conversation starters, and contextual suggestions that appear when the conversation stalls.
• **Maintaining context** — In long open-ended conversations, the AI needs to track what's been discussed. Surface conversation context visually — a sidebar with key topics, a summary of decisions made, or tags that indicate what areas have been covered.

**Hybrid Flows**

The most effective conversational interfaces blend these patterns based on the moment.

• **Start structured, open up** — An onboarding flow might be linear (gathering user preferences), then transition to open-ended interaction once the AI has enough context.
• **Open start, structured resolution** — A support conversation might start open-ended ("What's going on?"), then narrow into a branching troubleshooting flow once the issue is identified.
• **Periodic structure in open conversations** — In a long brainstorming session, the AI might periodically offer to summarize what's been discussed and organize ideas — introducing brief structure into an otherwise open flow.

sections.title: Map a Conversational Flow for a Support Bot
sections.content: Apply conversational flow patterns by designing the conversation architecture for an AI-powered support bot.

exercise.prompt:
You're designing a conversational support bot for a SaaS product that helps users manage their subscriptions. The bot needs to handle three main scenarios:

1. **Billing questions** — Users asking about charges, invoices, and payment methods
2. **Plan changes** — Users wanting to upgrade, downgrade, or cancel their subscription
3. **Technical issues** — Users experiencing bugs or needing help with features

Design the conversational flow by describing:

1. **Welcome state** — What does the user see when they open the support chat? What input mechanisms do you offer (free text, suggestions, or both)?
2. **Intent detection** — How does the conversation determine which of the three scenarios the user needs? Design both a guided path (using suggested responses) and a free-text path.
3. **One complete branch** — Pick one of the three scenarios and map out the full conversation flow including: the questions the bot asks, the decision points, the information it presents, and how the conversation resolves.
4. **Edge cases** — How do you handle: (a) a user whose issue spans two categories, (b) a user who wants to talk to a human, (c) a user who returns to the bot after a previous unresolved conversation?
5. **Message types** — For your complete branch, specify at least three different message types you'd use (text, cards, forms, quick replies, etc.) and explain why each is the right choice for that moment.

Focus on the conversation design, not the visual design. Describe what the bot says, what options it offers, and how the flow moves from state to state.
hints: For the welcome state, consider offering the three main categories as quick-reply buttons while also allowing free text — this covers both users who know what they need and users who aren't sure
Intent detection doesn't have to be binary — if the user types something ambiguous, the bot can ask a clarifying question rather than guessing
For your complete branch, think about what information the bot needs to collect and in what order — don't ask for everything upfront if some questions depend on earlier answers
Edge cases are where good conversational design separates from great — how you handle a user who says 'Actually, never mind, I have a different question' mid-flow shows how resilient your design is
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A welcome state that provides both guided (suggested responses) and unguided (free text) entry points with specific UI copy for the welcome message and suggestions. (2) An intent detection approach that handles both clear and ambiguous user inputs — including what the bot says when it's unsure which category applies. (3) A complete conversation branch with at least 4-5 conversational turns, showing specific bot messages, user response options, and decision points. (4) Thoughtful handling of all three edge cases with specific bot messages and flow transitions. (5) At least three different message types used in the complete branch, each with a clear rationale. Deduct points for generic bot messages that could apply to any product, for flows that don't handle user deviation, or for designs that only use text messages.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Conversational UI design is about creating structured interactions that feel natural:

• **Master the anatomy.** Every conversation UI needs a well-designed message thread, a thoughtful input area, and a system layer that provides context. These structural elements are the foundation that everything else builds on.

• **Match message types to content.** Plain text for explanations, cards for structured data, quick replies for decisions, inline forms for data collection. The right message format makes information instantly comprehensible; the wrong one buries it.

• **Design input for the moment.** Start with suggestions to lower the barrier to entry, use free text for complex queries, and switch to structured input when you need specific data. The input mechanism should guide users, not constrain them.

• **Choose flow patterns intentionally.** Linear flows for step-by-step processes, branching flows for decision-driven interactions, open-ended flows for exploration. The best interfaces blend patterns, transitioning between structure and freedom as the conversation evolves.
